mission.title: Install and Configure Aeraki Mesh on Kubernetes
mission.description: Aeraki is a service mesh that allows you to manage any layer-7 protocols, providing advanced traffic management capabilities for microservices. Installing Aeraki enables you to enhance your service mesh with support for various protocols beyond just HTTP.

steps.title: Add the Aeraki Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Aeraki Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add aeraki https://charts.aeraki.net
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Aeraki using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Aeraki in a new namespace called 'aeraki'.
```bash
helm install aeraki aeraki/aeraki --namespace aeraki --create-namespace --version 1.4.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Aeraki pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace aeraki
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the Aeraki deployment. Edit the deployment with:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment aeraki --namespace aeraki
```
Then add resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Aeraki will have the Aeraki pods running in the 'aeraki' namespace. You can verify this by running the command to check the pods, which should show them in a 'Running' state.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add aeraki https://charts.aeraki.net
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Aeraki release from the Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources managed by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall aeraki --namespace aeraki
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that may have been created during the installation. This ensures that all resources are cleaned up.
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep aeraki | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace aeraki
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'aeraki' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace aeraki
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Aeraki installation. This can be done by exporting the current configuration and resources.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace aeraki -o yaml > aeraki-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then upgrade the Aeraki installation to the desired version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade aeraki aeraki/aeraki --namespace aeraki --version 1.5.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check that the Aeraki pods are running with the new version and that the deployment is successful.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace aeraki
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Aeraki pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the specific pod to diagnose the issue.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace aeraki
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to a version conflict, ensure that the version specified is available in the repository. You can check available versions with:
```bash
helm search repo aeraki/aeraki --versions
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If services managed by Aeraki are not reachable, verify that the service endpoints are correctly configured and that the necessary network policies are in place.
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace aeraki
kubectl describe svc <service-name> --namespace aeraki
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources allocated
troubleshooting.description:
If Aeraki pods are failing due to insufficient resources, check the resource limits and requests configured in the deployment. Adjust them as necessary.
```bash
kubectl get deployment aeraki --namespace aeraki -o yaml
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
